📡 Stage Intel
🗺️ Race profiles, weather forecasts and detailed stage intel — updated as Le Tour approaches
Post Intel Anquetil only
🚴 Stage 1 — Route Intel4 Jul · Barcelona TTT
The Tour de France 2026 raises the curtain in Barcelona with a team time trial that is short, explosive and much more demanding than its figures suggest. The opening kilometres invite the big trains to run at very high speed, but the real test arrives in the final section with a double ascent of Montjuïc — first 0.9km at 5.5%, then 0.7km at 7.9%. Brief climbs, but difficult enough to punish any poor effort management. It won't be a decisive day, but it will offer a revealing first glimpse of the form of the teams in contention for the yellow jersey.
The Finish — Montjuïc Decides Everything
The closing 3.7km of this TTT are where the race is won and lost, and they are a world apart from everything that comes before them.
After a flat, technical sequence of tight turns and roundabouts through the Barcelona streets — including a sharp double bend with 4.4km and 4.1km to go that will test team cohesion at speed — the peloton hits the intermediate time check at 3.7km remaining. From this point, the roads change character entirely.
The route swings south and immediately pitches upward onto the Côte de Montjuïc — 900 metres at an average of 5.5%, but with a sting in the tail. The gradient stays manageable through the lower slopes before rearing up to 8.2% over the final 400 metres to the summit with 2.6km to go.
The summit is not the finish. After cresting the climb, a sharp turn leads west then south on a descending, twisting road — slightly technical, with a single bend at 1.7km to go — giving teams a brief moment to regroup before the final effort.
With 850 metres remaining, a gentle left-hander angles the race northeast and onto a narrow 6-metre wide road for the Côte du Stade Olimpic — 700 metres at 7.9%. It opens brutally: 9.0% for the first 200 metres, easing slightly to 6.4% through the middle, then grinding back up to 8.1% over the final 300 metres to the line.
The width of the road is worth noting — at 6 metres, eight riders riding abreast fills it completely. Teams that arrive at the base of the Olympic climb still intact will need to nominate their climbers fast. Any team that has fractured on Montjuïc faces a desperate final push up a narrow finishing ramp with nowhere to hide.
The stage totals 220 metres of elevation — modest on paper, but concentrated entirely where it matters most.
🚴 Stage 2 — Route Intel5 Jul · Tarragona → Barcelona
Stage 2 breaks with the idea of a comfortable opening week before it has even begun. From Tarragona back to Barcelona, the peloton faces a nervous, uneven stage with a classic finish. The Côte de Begues (6km at 5.9%) hardens the central section, before the race returns to a city that will once again act as judge and jury. A double ascent of Montjuïc — via the Olympic Stadium (700m at 7.9%) and the formidable Côte du Castell de Montjuïc (1.6km at 8.8%) — promises a ferocious finale where punchers and GC riders with a sharp kick will have every advantage.
The Finish — Montjuïc Again, Harder This Time
The stage concludes with two full laps of a 12.2km finishing circuit. Each lap begins with a technical descent before the road turns northeast, rolls over a short rise, then swings south for the climb.
The Côte du Castell de Montjuïc is 1.6km at 8.7% — steeper and longer than the Olympic climb from Stage 1. It builds progressively: 5.8% for the first 300m, 5.8%, 7.1% and 8.0% through the middle sections, before a savage 14.7% over the final 400 metres to the summit.
After cresting the summit, a slightly twisting descent leads southwest. With 700 metres remaining, a sharp right turn brings riders onto the run-in for the Côte du Estadi Olimpic — the same 700m at 7.9% finish used on Stage 1. A sharp right at the top of Montjuïc, then a brief winding descent to the final left-hander with 700m to go, and the line.
The stage totals 2,049 metres of elevation.
🚴 Stage 3 — Route Intel6 Jul · Granollers → Les Angles
Stage 3 brings the first contact with real mountains and the first summit finish of this edition. The route gradually builds before sharpening with the Sant Feliu de Codines (8.2km at 4.5%). The main act is the long Collada de Toses (9.4km at 6.6%) — the first major pass of the Tour — followed by the Col du Calvaire (11.5km at 4.2%) before the final summit ramp at Les Angles (1.7km at 6.7%).
Collada de Toses — First Mountain Test
The Collada de Toses is 9.4km at 6.6% — deceptive on paper, brutal in the legs. The opening kilometres are measured, but the last 3.9km ramp up to 9%, which is where the race will fracture for the first time in this Tour.
The Finish — Les Angles
The final climb — Côte des Angles–Pla del Mir, 1.7km at 6.7% — is twistier than it looks. It opens at 5.7%, 8.5% and 6.7% across the first three 400m segments, before easing slightly to 6.3% over the final 500m. Three hairpin turns in the lower section give way to a straight, narrow 5.5m wide road, a 90-degree bend 200 metres from the line, and then the finish.
The stage totals 3,940 metres of elevation — the first genuinely mountainous day of this Tour.
🚴 Stage 4 — Route Intel7 Jul · Carcassonne → Foix
A mid-mountain Pyrenean stage that rarely delivers on its modest billing — and rarely needs to. From Carcassonne, the terrain never allows a moment of rest. A succession of wearing passes accumulates fatigue before the finish at Foix: the Bedos Pass (3.4km at 4.4%), the Col de Paradis (5.6km at 4.3%), the demanding Col de Coudons (10.9km at 5.5%), and then the Col de Montségur (6.9km at 6.1%) as a final sting. The arrival at Foix invites a breakaway, but this is precisely the kind of day where inattention can cost a general classification contender dearly..
Col de Montségur — The Final Blow
The Col de Montségur (6.9km at 6.2%) arrives when legs are already heavy. It begins steadily — 3.1% for the first kilometre — before rearing up through the middle: 6.4%, 8.0%, 5.6%, 4.9% across successive kilometres, then hardening again to 7.7% and 7.8% over the final two kilometres to the summit.
The Finish — Foix
From the Col de Montségur summit, with 12.5km to go, a descent leads to Foix. Four kilometres from the line, a gentle rise — before the final 2km tips slightly downhill again, the last kilometre barely falling at 0.2%. The road is twisted but turnless into the city, until two sharp bends with 1,000m and 320m remaining funnel riders onto the 6-metre wide finishing straight.
The stage totals 2,784 metres of elevation.
🚴 Stage 5 — Route Intel8 Jul · Lannemezan → Pau
On paper, Stage 5 reads like a day off — and it may well be the last easy breath before the race turns savage. Between Lannemezan and Pau, a succession of short climbs and rhythm changes will gnaw at tired legs without ever threatening to crack the peloton. The sole rated difficulty is the Côte de Baleix (1.3km at 7.5%), though the real danger may come from the technical, urban finale in Pau. A day for sprinters — if they survive the chaos.
Côte de Baleix — Brief but Brutal
The Côte de Baleix (1.3km at 7.6%) arrives with enough road remaining to allow a regrouping, but its gradients are unforgiving for such a short climb: 6.7% for the first 300m, 9.1% through the middle, 9.3% before easing to 5.7% over the final 400m. Short, sharp, and well-placed to shatter any illusion of control.
The Finish — Pau
The approach to Pau is a test of positioning as much as legs. On the outskirts, a roundabout with 4.3km to go sends riders onto the ring road, which they follow west through another roundabout at 2.2km, then a sweeping bend at 2.1km. A sharp turn comes at 1.1km, followed by a roundabout at 900m, and one final bend at 560m before the 6.5-metre wide finishing straight opens up.
The stage totals 1,395 metres of elevation — the calmest day of the first week.
🚴 Stage 6 — Route Intel9 Jul · Pau → Gavarnie-Gèdre
This is where the Tour truly begins. Stage 6 out of Pau — a city inseparable from the race's history — delivers a high-mountain menu that commands full respect. After the appetisers of the Côte de Loucrup (1.8km at 7.2%) and the Côte de Mauvezin (3km at 6.4%), the Col d'Aspin (12km at 6.5%) marks the start of the serious business. Then comes the colossus: the Col du Tourmalet (17.2km at 7.3%), a pass that has destroyed legs, strategies and podium dreams in equal measure across a century of Tours. And still the racing is not over — the final climb to Gavarnie-Gèdre (18.7km at 3.7%) is long and irregular, and accumulated fatigue will make every extra percentage point feel like a wall.
Col du Tourmalet — The Colossus
The Col du Tourmalet rises 17.1km at 7.3% from Sainte-Marie-de-Campan to 2,115 metres — the highest paved pass in the French Pyrenees. The lower slopes are deceptively gentle, but the middle section tightens relentlessly with nine to ten percent gradients across kilometres 8 through 13, before the final two kilometres average 8.5% to the summit. La Mongie at 1,712m marks the halfway point — from there, there is nowhere left to hide.
The Finish — Gavarnie-Gèdre
From the Tourmalet summit, with 38.4km remaining, a descent drops southwest to Luz-Saint-Sauveur. At 19.8km to go, the final climb begins — the Gavarnie-Gèdre, 18.7km at 3.7%. Its modest average conceals a rhythm that refuses to let the legs recover: after 2km at 5–6%, there are 10km below 5%, then another 2km at 5–6%, then 2km easing to 2–4%, before the road stiffens again with 2km at 5.2% and 6.0% and a final 700m at 3.3%.
The road is almost straight throughout, with two hairpin bends through the village of Gèdre, before a near-straight run to two sharp 90-degree corners — one at 300m, one at 200m to go — funnelling riders onto the 40-metre long, 5-metre wide finishing straight.
The stage totals 4,149 metres of elevation — the hardest day of the first week.
🚴 Stage 7 — Route Intel 10 Jul · Hagetmau → Bordeaux
After the brutal first week, Stage 7 is the peloton's first genuine day of rest — though the sprinters' teams will have been working flat out. Hagetmau to Bordeaux through the flat Landes forest is as close to a controlled sprint as the Tour gets. The only obstacle is the Côte de Béguey (1.4km at 4.3%) with 20km remaining — not enough to split the bunch, but enough to test lead-out positioning in tired legs.
The Finish — Bordeaux, Place des Quinconces
The Côte de Béguey crests with 18km to go — teams have plenty of road to reorganise before Bordeaux. The approach through the southern suburbs brings three rapid turns in quick succession: the first at 5.4km to go, then at 4.8km, and a third at 4.75km as the road winds around a rectangular roundabout before crossing a bridge. A further swing at 3.9km from the line leads onto a wide boulevard running along the river — one gentle curve with 2km to go, then a right-hander onto the 560-metre finishing straight. The road is 6 metres wide and completely flat to the line. The sight lines are clear and there is nowhere to hide. This is a pure sprinters' stage.
🗠 Interactive Elevation Profile
Hagetmau175.1 km · +701mBordeaux
0–4% 4–7% 7–10% 10%+
The stage totals 701 metres of elevation — the easiest day of the Tour. Bordeaux has delivered some of cycling's great sprint finishes.
🚴 Stage 8 — Route Intel 11 Jul · Périgueux → Bergerac
Stage 8 is the sprinters' second chance in three days, but the road through the Dordogne is not as straightforward as it looks. Two categorised climbs — the Côte de Domme (3.6km at 3.3%) and the Côte du Buisson-de-Cadouin (2.9km at 4.3%) — arrive in the second half of the stage and will test any sprinter already operating on tired legs. The fast run-in to Bergerac offers little chance of a late surprise, but those intermediate sprint points will add an extra layer of tension throughout the day.
The Finish — Bergerac
After the Côte du Buisson-de-Cadouin crests with around 40km to go, the road descends into the Dordogne valley and tracks the river toward Bergerac. The approach feels straightforward until the final kilometres reveal a surprisingly technical finish. A wide boulevard leads to a roundabout at 4.7km to go, then the road crosses the river before threading another roundabout at 3.2km. A sharp turn comes at 1.4km, followed by two rapid direction changes in quick succession at 1,050m and 500m from the line before opening onto the 6-metre wide finishing straight. Five changes of direction in the final 4.7km will keep the lead-out trains honest — this is not the clean, unobstructed sprint it appeared on paper.
🗠 Interactive Elevation Profile
Périgueux180.4 km · +1,353mBergerac
0–4% 4–7% 7–10% 10%+
The stage totals 1,353 metres of elevation — moderate for a sprint stage, with the two climbs arriving late enough to cause real attrition.
🚴 Stage 9 — Route Intel 12 Jul · Malemort → Ussel
Stage 9 is the last day before the rest and the hardest non-mountain stage of the entire race. Malemort to Ussel through the volcanic Corrèze plateau delivers 3,244 metres of climbing without a single flat kilometre. No single ascent is a GC threat in isolation, but the accumulated fatigue of 185km of relentless saw-tooth terrain will be felt deeply on tired legs. The Suc au May (3.9km at 7.1%) and Mont Bessou (0.8km at 7.1%) in the final 80km are the flashpoints — whoever is still moving on Mont Bessou with 24km to go is in serious contention.
The Climbs — Corrèze Grinds Down the Peloton
The stage never relents. From the opening kilometres out of Malemort the road pitches and rolls through Corrèze, with the Puy Boubou (2.9km at 3.9%) and Côte de Naves (2.4km at 6.9%) arriving before the halfway mark to soften the legs. The Suc au May (3.9km at 7.1%) is the day's queen climb — a Cat 2 ascent that arrives at 105km and will shatter any pretence of a controlled peloton. The Côte de la Croix du Pey (4.8km at 5.7%) and Mont Bessou (0.8km at 7.1%) follow in the final 40km, the latter a short savage punch at 8.5% that arrives with 24km to go — too far from the finish to be decisive alone, but lethal as an accelerant on accumulated fatigue.
🗠 Interactive Elevation Profile
Malemort185.5 km · +3,244mUssel
0–4% 4–7% 7–10% 10%+
The Finish — Ussel
At 9.3km to go the road passes through Saint-Angel, the last village before Ussel. Two roundabouts punctuate the approach — the first before Saint-Angel, the second at 4.9km to go — before a final roundabout with 1km remaining channels riders onto the finishing straight. From there it is 500 metres of gently rising road, 5.5 metres wide, averaging 3.0% to the line. After 185km of relentless Corrèze climbing, that final 500-metre drag will feel like a wall.
The stage totals 3,244 metres of elevation — the hardest day of the first week, and a final examination before the rest day. This is where the race reshapes itself.

📋 Rules
🏆 Scoring
If selections are late please post immediately.
⏰ Deadlines
Pre-Tour selections — must be entered before 10:00 BST on 4 July.
Daily stage Picks — must be entered before 10:00 BST on each stage day. Fields lock at the deadline.
Final classifications — switch from current to final at 20:00 BST on 26 July.
🏆 Tiebreakers
If two or more players finish level on points at the end of Le Tour, the following tiebreakers apply in order:
1 Maillot Jaune — the player who correctly picked the Tour winner wins the tie.
2 GC Podium — the player with the most correct picks across GC 1st, 2nd and 3rd place wins the tie.
3 Jerseys — the player with the most correct jersey picks (Green, Polka Dot and White — Lanterne Rouge and French Stage Wins excluded) wins the tie.
4 Stage wins — the player with the most correct daily stage picks wins the tie.
5 Tie stands — if still level after all tiebreakers, there is more than one champion. 🏆🏆
⚖️ Sanctions
WADA and UCI do not have jurisdiction over this game, nor are their rules recognised. However, this game operates its own sanctions framework aligned with the cycling calendar.
Sanctions are not applied immediately, they are pending the outcome of the relevant UCI or WADA decision process. Once a decision is reached, the following rules apply:
Giro A sanction confirmed during the Giro applies to that rider's Giro results. A sanction confirmed after the Giro but before Le Tour starts also applies to the Giro, Le Tour is the deadline. Once Le Tour has started, subsequent sanctions relating to Giro riders no longer apply.
Le Tour A sanction confirmed during Le Tour applies to that rider's Tour results. A sanction confirmed after Le Tour but before La Vuelta starts also applies to Le Tour results. Once La Vuelta has started, subsequent sanctions no longer apply.
La Vuelta A sanction confirmed during La Vuelta applies to that rider's Vuelta results. A sanction confirmed after La Vuelta but before Il Lombardia (the last Monument of the season) also applies to La Vuelta results. After Il Lombardia, subsequent La Vuelta sanctions no longer apply.
Where a sanction is upheld under these rules, points will be recalibrated accordingly for all affected players.
🛡️ Spoiler Shield
The 🛡️ Spoiler Shield button sits flush against the 👁️ All Picks tab in the navigation bar, so it is clear they are associated. Tap it to activate, when on it turns red.
Per-player — each player has their own independent shield. A player activating their shield does not affect the other players. The shield shown always reflects whoever is currently identified as themselves in the Stages tab.
When active — stage results and picks in the All Picks table are hidden from the moment of activation onwards. Past results (stages already revealed before activation) remain fully visible.
When deactivated — all results become visible simultaneously, including any stages that accumulated whilst the shield was active.
The Spoiler Shield only hides stage results in All Picks. It does not affect the Leaderboard, Points Breakdown, or Live Race Standings. Simply avoid clicking those tabs if you do not want to see them.
The shield persists across page loads and browser restarts. It remains active until the player explicitly taps the button to have it deactivated. Refreshing the page will not turn it off.
📢 Media Centre
The 📢 Media tab is a shared message board for all players.
Select your name from the dropdown, type your message, and tap Post. Messages are visible to all players in real time.
Anquetil posts are used to notify players of any changes or updates, scoring corrections, rule clarifications, technical issues etc.
🔒 Privacy & Pick Visibility
✏️A player can change their stage pick any number of times before the deadline. The most recent entry is what counts. Once the deadline passes the field locks and no further changes are possible.
🔒Before the deadline — a player's stage pick is visible only to themselves. If another player views that player's stages, they see "Pick hidden until the deadline".
🔓After the deadline — the pick is revealed to all players automatically in the Stages tab and the All Picks table.
🎽Pre-tour picks — all pre-tour selections remain hidden from other players until 10:00 on 4 July, at which point all selections are revealed simultaneously.
🚫No editing other players' picks — the app only allows a player to edit their own picks. Attempts to enter a pick under another player's name are blocked.
🏮DNS (Did Not Sign-in) — if a stage pick field is empty when the deadline passes, a DNS is recorded automatically. A DNS scores 0 points. If a Joker was active on that stage it is cancelled and cannot be used again.
